patterns.what_to_look_for: Object storage (S3 bucket, GCS bucket, Azure blob container) configured for public/anonymous read or list, exposing data the app references via direct or signed URLs. Look for bucket hostnames in HTML/JS/API responses and test anonymous access plus directory listing.
source_indicators: S3 bucket policy with Principal: "*" and s3:GetObject / s3:ListBucket
aws_s3_bucket_acl acl = "public-read" or block_public_acls = false in Terraform
AllUsers / AllAuthenticatedUsers granted on GCS objectAdmin or legacyObjectReader
Azure container with publicAccess: Blob or Container
CDN/origin config pointing at *.s3.amazonaws.com / storage.googleapis.com with no signing
blackbox_indicators: Asset URLs like https://<bucket>.s3.amazonaws.com/ or storage.googleapis.com/<bucket>/ in page source
Anonymous GET on bucket root returns ListBucketResult XML with <Key> entries
200 OK on objects without any AWS Signature / X-Amz-* params or SAS token
S3 error AccessDenied vs NoSuchBucket distinguishing existence
Predictable object keys (uploads/<id>.pdf) enumerable without auth
evidence_required: HTTP request/response showing anonymous 200 on a non-public object containing user/business data
ListBucketResult XML proving anonymous listing, or proof keys are guessable
The exact bucket name + region + object key retrieved
Classification of leaked data (PII, backups, internal docs)
false_positive_checks: Confirm object is genuinely sensitive, not an intentionally public static asset (logo, css, marketing image)
Verify access is truly anonymous (drop all auth headers/cookies/signed params and re-request)
Distinguish CloudFront/OAC-fronted public delivery from raw open bucket
Check bucket actually belongs to target org, not a shared third-party CDN
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send an unauthenticated GET to the bucket root and one referenced object URL with all credentials stripped, recording status and whether listing/content returns.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as data exposure: name the bucket, sample sensitive keys retrieved anonymously, and the exact misconfigured policy/ACL to fix.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254, metadata.google.internal, Azure IMDS) reachable through an SSRF-capable feature (URL fetcher, webhook, image/PDF proxy, importer) allowing theft of instance IAM/STS credentials.
source_indicators: Server-side fetch of user-supplied URL (axios/requests/curl) without host allowlist or IP-range deny
No block of link-local 169.254.0.0/16 / fd00:ec2::254 in URL validation
Webhook/callback URL, RSS importer, SVG/PDF renderer, or avatar-by-URL feature
IMDSv1 in use (no metadata hop-limit / token requirement) in instance/launch-template config
Redirect-following enabled on outbound fetch (maxRedirects > 0) without re-validating target
blackbox_indicators: A parameter that accepts a URL and the server fetches it (out-of-band callback observed)
Differential responses/timing when pointing fetch at 169.254.169.254 vs a dead IP
Ability to coerce request to http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ and see directory listing reflected
GCP metadata reachable only with Metadata-Flavor: Google header injectable via fetch
DNS-rebinding or redirect (302 to 169.254.169.254) honored by the fetcher
evidence_required: Response proving metadata reached: meta-data listing, or iam/security-credentials/<role> JSON
For confirmed impact: a retrieved AccessKeyId/SecretAccessKey/Token (redacted in report) or its presence
The exact SSRF request (parameter + payload URL) and the relayed metadata content
IMDS version indicator (v1 unauthenticated success vs v2 token required)
false_positive_checks: Confirm fetch is server-side, not browser-side (response actually returned/observed by server)
Rule out a mock/stub metadata service in non-cloud or local dev environment
Verify 169.254 is not blocked at egress (response may be a generic error, not real metadata)
Check whether IMDSv2 hop-limit blocks the proxy path (token retrieval may fail)
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Point the URL-fetch feature at http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (and iam/security-credentials/) and capture whether the listing returns, without using any retrieved credential.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report SSRF-to-metadata as critical: show the metadata listing reached and credential path exposed; recommend IMDSv2-enforce + egress block of link-local ranges.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Over-permissive IAM role / service account attached to the running workload or handed to clients: wildcard actions/resources, AdministratorAccess, or privilege-escalation-enabling permissions (iam:PassRole, iam:CreatePolicyVersion, sts:AssumeRole *).
source_indicators: IAM policy with Action: "*" or service:* and Resource: "*"
Attached AWS managed policy AdministratorAccess / PowerUserAccess on app role
iam:PassRole + lambda/ec2/glue create permissions enabling escalation
GCP roles/owner or roles/editor bound to a service account, or roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator broadly
Trust policy Principal: "*" with sts:AssumeRole and no external-id/condition
Long-lived static access keys checked into config / IaC instead of role assumption
blackbox_indicators: Leaked/recovered credentials (from CLOUD-002, JS, or git) that authenticate to cloud APIs
sts get-caller-identity succeeds and role name implies broad scope
API responses or signed URLs revealing the workload can act outside its function (e.g., read unrelated buckets)
Client-delivered temporary credentials (Cognito/STS) with scope broader than the feature needs
evidence_required: The attached policy document or enumerated effective permissions
Benign proof of over-permission: a read-only API call succeeding that the role should not have (e.g., list unrelated resource)
Caller identity (ARN / service account email) tying permissions to the app
A concrete escalation or blast-radius path described (which permission grants what)
false_positive_checks: Confirm permissions are actually broader than the function requires, not justified by the workload
Check for restricting Conditions (aws:SourceIp, aws:PrincipalTag, resource tags) that narrow the wildcard
Verify Service Control Policies / permission boundaries don't already cap the effective grant
Ensure recovered credentials are live, not expired/rotated test keys
patterns.suggested_validation_task: With any recovered workload credentials, run only a read-only identity/listing call (e.g., sts:GetCallerIdentity, iam:GetPolicy, list a bucket) to demonstrate scope without modifying anything.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as excessive privilege: show effective permissions, a benign over-reach call, and the least-privilege policy that replaces the wildcard.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Cloud/secret material hardcoded in source, config, client bundles, or env exposure: AWS keys, GCP service-account JSON, connection strings, or an exposed environment dump endpoint.
source_indicators: Strings matching AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16} / ASIA... with adjacent 40-char secret
GCP service account JSON with "private_key": "-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----" committed
Connection strings with embedded password (postgres://user:pass@host, mongodb+srv://...)
.env / config.yaml / appsettings.json with live secrets tracked in git
Secrets baked into client JS bundle, Dockerfile ENV, or Terraform default variable
blackbox_indicators: Secrets visible in served JS source maps / webpack bundles (grep for AKIA, apikey, secret)
Exposed /actuator/env, /debug, /info, phpinfo(), or Next.js __NEXT_DATA__ leaking env vars
Verbose error/stack trace echoing DB connection string or token
Publicly readable .git/config or .env via path traversal / open dir
evidence_required: Exact location (file:line, URL, or bundle path) and redacted snippet of the secret
Proof of validity: a benign auth check confirming the credential is live (without using its powers)
Scope of what the secret unlocks (which service/account)
Confirmation the secret is exposed to an untrusted party (public URL / shipped bundle / public repo)
false_positive_checks: Distinguish real secrets from placeholders/examples (AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE, dummy, changeme, test)
Verify the credential is not already revoked/rotated (validity check returns unauthorized)
Confirm it is a backend secret, not a publishable key intended to be public (e.g., Stripe pk_, Firebase web config)
Check exposure surface is truly untrusted, not an internal-only build artifact
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Run a single non-mutating validity probe for the credential type (e.g., sts:GetCallerIdentity for AWS, tokeninfo for GCP) to confirm it is live, then stop.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as secret exposure: cite location, prove validity with one read-only check, and require immediate rotation + removal from history/bundle.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Infrastructure/admin/debug services bound to a public interface without auth: Kubernetes API/kubelet, etcd, Docker API, Redis/Mongo/Elasticsearch, or management dashboards (Kibana, Consul, Spark, Jenkins, Grafana, Prometheus, Actuator).
source_indicators: Service bind address 0.0.0.0 for a backing store (redis bind 0.0.0.0, mongod bindIp 0.0.0.0) with no auth
Docker daemon -H tcp://0.0.0.0:2375 (unauthenticated remote API)
Kubernetes --anonymous-auth=true / --insecure-port set, or kubelet --authorization-mode=AlwaysAllow
Ingress/Service of type LoadBalancer exposing admin port (9200, 8500, 2379, 10250, 6443)
Spring management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=* without securing /actuator
blackbox_indicators: Open admin ports: 2375/2376 (Docker), 6443/10250 (k8s), 2379 (etcd), 9200 (ES), 8500 (Consul), 6379 (Redis)
Unauthenticated GET /version, /api/v1/namespaces (k8s), /_cat/indices (ES), /v1/agent/members (Consul) returns 200
Redis responds to PING/INFO without AUTH; Mongo allows isMaster without creds
/actuator, /actuator/heapdump, Jenkins /script, Kibana, Grafana login-bypass dashboards reachable publicly
evidence_required: HTTP/protocol response from the admin service proving unauthenticated access (version/listing banner)
The exposed host:port and service identification
A benign read demonstrating data/control reach (e.g., list namespaces, INFO, _cat/indices)
Confirmation it is reachable from outside the trust boundary (public IP / internet egress)
false_positive_checks: Confirm the port is reachable externally, not just on localhost/private subnet from the scan host
Verify no auth is required (some return a banner but reject commands without creds)
Distinguish a honeypot/decoy or read-only health proxy from the real service
Check whether mTLS/network policy actually gates it despite the open port appearing
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send one unauthenticated read-only request to the exposed admin port (e.g., Docker GET /version, k8s GET /version, Redis INFO) and capture the banner/response without issuing any state-changing command.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as exposed infrastructure service: name host:port, show the unauthenticated banner/listing, and recommend auth + network restriction (no 0.0.0.0 bind).

patterns.what_to_look_for: Insecure container/Kubernetes runtime configuration that escalates a foothold to host/cluster: privileged containers, hostPath/hostNetwork/hostPID mounts, overly broad RBAC, or default service-account token auto-mounted with cluster-wide rights.
source_indicators: Pod spec securityContext.privileged: true or allowPrivilegeEscalation: true / runAsUser: 0
hostPath volume mounting / or /var/run/docker.sock, or hostNetwork/hostPID/hostIPC: true
RBAC ClusterRole with verbs ["*"] resources ["*"], or binding system:anonymous / default SA to cluster-admin
automountServiceAccountToken not disabled and SA bound to broad roles
Missing capabilities drop / no seccomp/AppArmor (securityContext lacks readOnlyRootFilesystem, capabilities.drop)
blackbox_indicators: From a compromised pod: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token present and accepted by API
kubectl auth can-i --list (via API) shows broad verbs for the mounted SA
Container can see host filesystem (/host mount) or docker.sock, or is uid 0 with full caps
Exposed kubelet (10250) /pods or /exec reachable enabling container command run
evidence_required: The pod/RBAC manifest fragment showing the dangerous setting
Benign proof: API call with the mounted SA token succeeding (e.g., self subject-access-review or list pods)
Demonstrated reach (host mount visible, or SA can read secrets in other namespaces) without altering state
Identification of the cluster/namespace and the escalation path
false_positive_checks: Confirm the privileged/hostPath setting is on a real workload, not a one-off admin/CNI/CSI system pod expected to have it
Verify the SA token actually grants broad access (run a can-i check), not a narrowly-scoped role
Check PodSecurity admission / OPA-Gatekeeper isn't already blocking the manifest at deploy time
Distinguish a lab/dev cluster from the in-scope production cluster
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Using the in-pod service-account token, issue a single read-only SelfSubjectAccessReview or 'list pods' API call to enumerate granted scope, taking no mutating cluster action.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as container-escape/RBAC risk: show the insecure manifest setting and the benign access proof, and recommend dropping privilege, PodSecurity enforcement, and least-privilege RBAC.
